title: Cat Wobbly Walking: Understanding Ataxia and Why It's an Emergency
meta_desc: Is your cat walking like they are drunk? Learn about ataxia in cats, why it requires urgent veterinary care, and how to assist your vet.
content_html:
TL;DR: A sudden wobbly or "drunken" gait in cats is a high-priority medical emergency that requires immediate veterinary attention to diagnose potentially life-threatening causes like toxin ingestion or neurological trauma.

What is ataxia and why is my cat walking like they are drunk?

When a cat walks with a wobbly, uncoordinated, or "drunken" gait, it is clinically referred to as ataxia. This condition occurs when there is a lack of muscle coordination caused by a disruption in the sensory system, the inner ear, or the brain. You may notice your cat swaying, stumbling, or even falling over while trying to walk normally.

Is it an emergency if my cat is suddenly wobbly or off-balance?

  • The urgency level for a sudden wobbly gait is High.
  • A cat that suddenly loses its balance could be suffering from a variety of serious conditions, including toxin ingestion (such as lilies or antifreeze), neurological trauma, a stroke, or a severe inner ear infection.
  • Because some of these causes can be life-threatening if left untreated, you should contact an emergency veterinarian immediately.

How can taking photos or videos of my cat’s movement help the veterinarian?

  • If it is safe to do so, capture a short video of your cat's movement to share with the veterinary triage team.
  • The specific way your cat stumbles—such as leaning to one side, circling, or showing rhythmic eye movements—helps the vet determine if the issue is vestibular (inner ear) or central (brain-related).
  • Providing video evidence helps the clinic prepare for your arrival and speeds up the triage process.

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Ataxia in cats can be a sign of several underlying conditions. Glycoproteinoses, particularly α-Mannosidosis, has been seen in cats and may cause retinal and skeletal abnormalities, along with neurologic deficits, where cerebellar signs are the most consistent feature. Mucopolysaccharidosis is also primarily a disorder of cats. While rare, congenital hypomyelination can occur in cats, though it is more commonly seen in certain dog breeds. Progressive myelopathy can cause spastic paraparesis and ataxia. The severity and progression of ataxia can vary, but it is important to identify the underlying cause to determine the best course of action.

Chapter: Neurology, Genetics

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1235)

faqs.question: Can a wobbly gait be caused by something my cat ate?
faqs.answer: Yes, many toxins, including certain houseplants, human medications, and chemicals, can cause neurological symptoms like wobbliness.

faqs.question: Should I wait a few hours to see if it improves?
faqs.answer: No. Because ataxia can indicate a rapidly progressing neurological issue or poisoning, waiting can lead to permanent damage or a worse prognosis.

faqs.question: What is the difference between ataxia and weakness?
faqs.answer: Weakness is a lack of physical strength to move, whereas ataxia is a lack of coordination where the cat has the strength to move but cannot control their placement accurately.
